title: The Mess Christmas Saves
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-10-23 -PM- The Mess Christmas Saves - Genesis 6_5-8 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: What Has Genesis 6 to Do with Christmas?
subpoints: This passage describes the mess that Christmas came to save
It speaks of what led to the first advent of the Lord Jesus Christ

outline.title: What Does It Mean for God to Be Sorry?
subpoints: God uses human language and forms to communicate truths we can understand
God is impassible — His affections do not change as ours do
What changed was humanity's category, not God's disposition toward those categories
God always hated evil and always loved righteousness

outline.title: The End of Love for God in the Heart of Man
subpoints: Humanity was created to love God — every motive, desire, and action shaped by love for God
Genesis 6 signals the end of love for God in the heart of humanity
Every human being is hardwired to love — the question is what we love
Self-love always replaces love for God: the basic sin from which all others spring

outline.title: God's Holy Response: The Flood
subpoints: God sent the flood as an act of holy justice, not ugly vengeance
Self-love had caused the world to explode into chaos
The same self-love still rules today

outline.title: Grace in the Midst of Judgment: Noah
subpoints: Noah was no different from the others, yet God placed His saving favor on him
Not because of who Noah was, but because of who God is
From Noah comes Abraham, from Abraham comes the covenant of blessing to all nations
God destroyed many in justice so that He could save a multitude through grace

outline.title: The Advent: God Takes the Punishment Himself
subpoints: Instead of sending another flood, God sent His Son
Jesus lived perfectly, died as the ultimate sacrifice, and rose again
He paid the penalty for sin, breaking the power of sin on the cross
His presence in us is breaking the presence of sin through sanctifying grace

outline.title: Application: What Love Sits on the Throne of Your Heart?
subpoints: We must be honest: our hearts are still prone to love self over God
The Advent is both a confrontation with our sin and a celebration of hope
Questions for reflection: What grieves your heart? What grieves God's heart? When did you die to self this week?

summary: In this Advent evening sermon, Pastor Lyro takes a striking detour from typical Christmas texts to Genesis 6:5-8, arguing that this passage of judgment and grace describes the very mess that Christmas came to save. He begins by addressing what it means for God to be sorry, explaining that while God does not experience changing emotions as we do, He uses human language to communicate the gravity of humanity's change — moving from righteousness to wickedness. Humanity was originally created to love God, with every action flowing from that love. Genesis 6 signals the end of that love in the human heart. What replaced it is self-love — the root sin from which all other sins spring. Murder, violence, greed, gossip, adultery — all are rooted in love of self rather than love of God. By the time of Genesis 6, self-love had exploded the world into chaos. God's response was the flood — an act of holy justice. Yet in sovereign grace, God placed His saving favor on Noah and his family, not because of who Noah was, but because of who God is. From Noah came Abraham, from Abraham came the covenant of blessing to all nations, and from that covenant came the Lord Jesus Christ. This is what the Advent is truly about: God, grieved by humanity's betrayal but motivated by even greater personal love, did not send another flood but sent His Son. Jesus took upon Himself the punishment that justice demanded, breaking the power of sin on the cross. The sermon closes with searching questions for personal reflection: Is your heart grieved by the same things that grieve God? In what ways did self-love rule your life this week? The Advent is a call to honest self-examination and a glorious celebration of the hope that is ours in the Christ child.
transcript:
## Introduction: What Has Genesis 6 to Do with Christmas?

As we continue in our Advent series, let us turn to that classic Christmas passage — Genesis chapter 6. Reading verses 5 through 8:

"Then the Lord saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually. And the Lord was sorry that He had made man on the earth, and He was grieved in His heart. So the Lord said, 'I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth, both man and beast, creeping thing and birds of the air, for I am sorry that I have made them.' But Noah found grace in the eyes of the Lord."

You may ask: what in the world does this passage have to do with Christmas? Well, this passage speaks of the mess that Christmas saves — the mess that Christ came to save. What we read here is what led to the first advent of the Lord Jesus Christ. This is a very graphic, very specific, and yet all-inclusive passage.

## What Does It Mean for God to Be Sorry?

Before we get into the passage and into Christmas, we have to briefly consider what it means for God to be sorry. God uses language we can understand, even though language is limited to describe God fully. There is no human language able to describe God fully because language is a finite, created instrument and therefore cannot precisely describe the One who is infinite.

He uses human forms to get a concept across — the arm of the Lord, the mouth of the Lord, the hand of the Lord. We get into trouble when we start to attribute human dimensions to God. He describes Himself with human emotions, even though He doesn't feel things like we do. Our emotions are changing — we can be angry and then not angry. God is not like that. His passions and affections are the same, without changing.

And yet God as a personal God is really grieved by sin. God is so closely involved with the life of His people that this repenting and regretting — this change of mind — described in the passage is consistent with God's revealed and immutable mode of operation. God always hates evil and always loves righteousness. What changed was not God — what changed was humanity. Adam and Eve were righteous. He loved them. Then they fell and became wicked. God always hated the wicked and always loved the righteous. So the author of Genesis is putting in human terms the difference in God's approach to people who have changed categories.

## The End of Love for God in the Heart of Man

Now, to understand the significance of this passage, we need to understand the original intent of creation. Humanity was created to love God — to live in an intimate relationship with the Creator that would shape every motive, every desire, every choice, every word, and every action. If at any moment before the fall you asked Adam why he was doing what he was doing, his answer would be: "Because I love my God."

But then we get to Genesis 6, and it's clear that something went very, very wrong. Love for God no longer ruled the heart of man. Genesis 6:5-8 signals the end of love for God in the heart of man.

This does not stop humanity from being lovers. Every human being is hardwired to love. The question now becomes: what do we love? In essence, the love that rules our hearts changed. At the deepest, most profound level of our existence, we love — and we love either God or ourselves. Those are the only two objects of love in the Scriptures. As Paul says in 2 Corinthians 5:15: "He died for all, that those who live should no longer live for themselves but for Him who died for them and rose again."

The thing that always replaces love for God is love of self. And the thing that leads to an endless catalog of sin is always love of self. That is the basic sin. You and I find a way to insert ourselves into the center of our world. We are obsessed with our will. We want to be sovereign over our own lives. We want to make the rules. We are addicted to our own pleasure and happiness.

What makes marriage hard? You have two individuals who love themselves — self-love quickly escalates to conflict unless the Lord intervenes. What makes parenting so hard? You have given birth to children who love themselves. As soon as you give them rules that rub against their desires, that self-love escalates to conflict. Brothers and sisters, every sin is rooted in self-love. Every act of murder, every act of violence, every moment of greed, every word of gossip and slander, every act of adulterous desire is rooted in self-love.

By Genesis 6, the world had exploded into chaos because self-love ruled the heart of every man, woman, and child. The same self-love still rules today.

## God's Holy Response: The Flood

And the heart of our Lord was grieved, because His beautiful creation — designed to live in harmony with Him, designed to live in eternal love with Him — had betrayed Him. And in verse 7, God said: "I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth, both man and beast, creeping thing and birds of the air, for I am sorry that I have made them."

Creation always gets the short end of the stick — we mess up and the birds pay for it. But God, who lovingly and personally designed every element in creation, sent the waters of the flood to wipe the earth clean. God had every right to do so. The flood came and destroyed humanity — why? Because of self-love.

## Grace in the Midst of Judgment: Noah

Praise and glory that the story doesn't end there. Verse 8: "But Noah found grace in the eyes of the Lord."

In an act of sovereign grace, God placed His saving favor on Noah and his family. Noah was no different from the other people — still involved in self-love. Yet God placed His love, His grace, His favor upon Noah and delivered Noah and his family from the judgment. Not because of who Noah was, but because of who God is.

If you read past the covenant with Noah in Genesis 9 and keep on reading, you'll come across a very familiar name. In chapter 11, it says, "Now Terah lived seventy years, and begot Abram" — and God changes Abram's name to Abraham, and makes a covenant with him, promising to bless all the nations of the earth: "In your seed, all the nations of the earth shall be blessed." And it is because of that covenant with Abraham that you and I are here today in faith in Jesus Christ — all because Noah found grace in the sight of God.

God destroyed the population of the world in the flood as an act of justice. He saved eight souls in the family of Noah, so that from that remnant He would have a multitude of people following Him — a multitude that cannot be numbered — far greater than those destroyed in the flood.

## The Advent: God Takes the Punishment Himself

And this is what the Advent is all about. God was personally grieved by the betrayal of His creation, but motivated by even greater personal love. He provided a Savior — instead of sending a flood to destroy the earth, He sent His Son to be exposed to all the harsh realities of life in a fallen world. As if God were saying: "I tried punishing the people — that's not going to do it. I will take the punishment of justice upon Myself, so that My people can have salvation and experience My love."

Despite daily pain and temptation, Jesus lived perfectly to provide the ultimate sacrifice that we could never provide. And in so doing, He paid the penalty for sin — crushing the wrath of God, restoring hope to the entire universe. On the cross, the Messiah broke the power of sin forever. And being present with us daily through His Spirit, He is breaking the presence of sin in our lives. There is still a battle in us because of the presence of sin, but it is being eradicated by sanctifying grace as we continue to be indwelt by the Spirit of God.

## Application: What Love Sits on the Throne of Your Heart?

So when you look at Genesis 6, you need to be honest. There are times when your thoughts are shaped by love for God — but not always. There are times when your desires flow from a heart that loves the Lord — but not always. You and I gave evidence this week that the war of love is still going on in our hearts. Not because I know something specific you did — but because the Bible says that's the case.

What love sits on the throne of your heart? Love for God, or love for self?

In this Advent season, you need to embrace the sad reality that your heart is still prone to betray the Lord. And realizing that is a great thing — because then you can do something about it. But the Advent is also a glorious celebration of the hope that is ours, represented by that baby in a manger who has come on a mission to rescue and deliver us. There will be a day when every microbe of sin will be destroyed from our bodies, when every ounce of sin will be taken away from our souls — and we will live completely inside God's boundaries and live for His glory forever and ever.

As you think about the need for the Advent this week, ask yourself: What are some things that grieve your heart? Do the same things grieve the Lord's heart? In what ways this week did you allow love for self to replace love for God? And in what ways did you die to self and act out of love for God?

May the Lord give us freedom to enjoy God's love in Jesus Christ as we think about His birth — and as we look at our own hearts, to eradicate love for self and replace it with love for God.

Father, thank you that you are God. We speak to you. We thank you for the history of redemption, from Genesis on, speaking of the coming of our Messiah, and from the cross on, speaking about the return of our Savior. We pray that you search our hearts. Reveal to us where love of self is present. Grant us repentance. Enable us to replace that love of self with a love of you. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
